title: Reflection
text: This module pushed me into unfamiliar technical territory — and that discomfort turned out to be exactly what I needed.

title: Reflection
text: This module pushed me into unfamiliar technical territory — and that discomfort turned out to be exactly what I needed.

title: Reflection
text:
Git and Version Control
Git was the skill I underestimated most. In the beginning, I treated it as a backup tool — something you use when a project is finished. That mindset fell apart quickly. My first few commits were bulk uploads with vague messages like “added files”, which meant the history was useless. Once I started committing incrementally — one logical change at a time, with a message that explained why, not just what — the repository became genuinely readable.
The discipline of writing good commit messages is surprisingly hard. It forces you to think clearly about what you are actually changing and why. That is a thinking skill, not just a Git skill.






ImportantBiggest Early Mistake



I accidentally staged my entire docs/ folder before understanding that it should only contain rendered output. Cleaning that up taught me more about .gitignore than any tutorial did.

title: Reflection
text:
Quarto
Quarto’s separation of content and configuration was initially confusing. The _quarto.yml file controls so much — navbar, theme, output directory, footer — and a single indentation error breaks the whole build. I spent more time than I’d like to admit debugging YAML whitespace.
Once it clicked, though, the power was obvious. Writing in Markdown with executable Python chunks means analysis and presentation live in the same file. There is no copying charts into slides, no broken links between a script and a report. Everything renders together, reproducibly.

title: Reflection
text:
Python for Data Analysis
My Python background was functional but shallow before this module. Working with pandas for financial data — cleaning, resampling, computing rolling averages — required me to understand the data structures properly, not just copy patterns.
Plotly was a genuine step up from static charts. Interactivity changes how you explore data. A chart you can zoom into and hover over reveals things a static PNG hides.

title: Reflection
text:
Web Design and SCSS
I had never written SCSS before. The scss:defaults and scss:rules sections required understanding how Sass variables cascade into Bootstrap’s theming system. Getting the navbar colour to actually change — not just appear to change — required reading how $navbar-bg feeds into the compiled CSS.

title: Reflection
text:
What I Would Do Differently
If I were starting over, I would set up the .gitignore, .nojekyll, and _quarto.yml properly on day one before writing a single page. Retrofitting configuration onto an existing structure wastes time and creates messy commits.
I would also start the Python projects earlier. Debugging plotly rendering inside Quarto took longer than expected because the output depends on the execution environment being configured correctly.

title: Reflection
text:
Looking Forward
These skills connect directly to what I want to do professionally. A business analyst who can version-control their work, publish reproducible analyses, and present interactive visualisations is significantly more capable than one who emails Excel files. This portfolio is the beginning of that professional presence — something I intend to keep building long after this module ends.





flowchart LR
  A[Raw Data] --> B[Python & pandas]
  B --> C[Exploratory Analysis]
  C --> D[Plotly Visualisation]
  D --> E[Quarto Report]
  E --> F[GitHub Pages]

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
This project performs a full sales analytics workflow on a simulated retail dataset covering 12 months of transactions across four product categories and three regions. The analysis identifies top-performing segments, seasonal patterns, and provides a simple revenue forecast.






NoteDataset



Synthetically generated to reflect realistic retail sales distributions. The same analytical approach applies directly to real CRM or ERP exports.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
This project performs a full sales analytics workflow on a simulated retail dataset covering 12 months of transactions across four product categories and three regions. The analysis identifies top-performing segments, seasonal patterns, and provides a simple revenue forecast.






NoteDataset



Synthetically generated to reflect realistic retail sales distributions. The same analytical approach applies directly to real CRM or ERP exports.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Data Generation


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

np.random.seed(7)

months = pd.date_range(start='2024-01-01', periods=12, freq='MS')
categories = ['Electronics', 'Clothing', 'Home & Garden', 'Food & Beverage']
regions = ['Dublin', 'Cork', 'Galway']

rows = []
for month in months:
    for cat in categories:
        for region in regions:
            base = {'Electronics': 45000, 'Clothing': 28000,
                    'Home & Garden': 18000, 'Food & Beverage': 32000}[cat]
            seasonal = 1 + 0.15 * np.sin((month.month - 3) * np.pi / 6)
            noise = np.random.uniform(0.88, 1.12)
            revenue = round(base * seasonal * noise, 2)
            units = int(revenue / np.random.uniform(25, 85))
            rows.append({
                'Month': month,
                'Category': cat,
                'Region': region,
                'Revenue': revenue,
                'Units': units
            })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
print(f"Dataset: {len(df)} records")
print(df.groupby('Category')['Revenue'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).apply(lambda x: f"€{x:,.0f}"))


Dataset: 144 records
Category
Electronics        €1,611,677
Food & Beverage    €1,171,384
Clothing           €1,002,548
Home & Garden        €651,303
Name: Revenue, dtype: str

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Monthly Revenue Trend


Code
monthly = df.groupby('Month')['Revenue'].sum().reset_index()

fig = px.bar(
    monthly,
    x='Month',
    y='Revenue',
    title='Total Monthly Revenue — 2024',
    template='plotly_white',
    color='Revenue',
    color_continuous_scale='Blues',
    text_auto='.2s'
)

fig.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    yaxis_tickprefix='€',
    coloraxis_showscale=False,
    xaxis_title='Month',
    yaxis_title='Revenue (€)'
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            
Total monthly revenue across all categories and regions — clear seasonal uplift in summer months.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Revenue by Category


Code
cat_totals = df.groupby('Category')['Revenue'].sum().reset_index()

fig2 = px.pie(
    cat_totals,
    names='Category',
    values='Revenue',
    title='Annual Revenue Share by Product Category',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Pastel,
    hole=0.4
)

fig2.update_traces(textposition='outside', textinfo='percent+label')
fig2.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18
)

fig2.show()


                            
                                            
Electronics dominates total revenue, contributing over 37% of annual sales.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Regional Performance


Code
regional = df.groupby(['Region', 'Category'])['Revenue'].sum().reset_index()

fig3 = px.bar(
    regional,
    x='Region',
    y='Revenue',
    color='Category',
    barmode='group',
    title='Revenue by Region and Category',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
    text_auto='.2s'
)

fig3.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    yaxis_tickprefix='€',
    xaxis_title='Region',
    yaxis_title='Revenue (€)',
    legend_title='Category'
)

fig3.show()


                            
                                            
Dublin leads in all categories. Cork performs strongly in Food & Beverage. Galway shows growth potential.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Simple Revenue Forecast (Next 3 Months)


Code
monthly['Rolling_Avg'] = monthly['Revenue'].rolling(3).mean()

forecast_months = pd.date_range(start='2025-01-01', periods=3, freq='MS')
last_avg = monthly['Rolling_Avg'].iloc[-1]
growth_rate = 0.02
forecast_values = [last_avg * (1 + growth_rate) ** i for i in range(1, 4)]

forecast_df = pd.DataFrame({'Month': forecast_months, 'Revenue': forecast_values})
forecast_df['Type'] = 'Forecast'
monthly['Type'] = 'Actual'

combined = pd.concat([monthly[['Month', 'Revenue', 'Type']], forecast_df])

fig4 = px.line(
    combined,
    x='Month',
    y='Revenue',
    color='Type',
    markers=True,
    title='Revenue Trend with 3-Month Forecast',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_map={'Actual': '#2980b9', 'Forecast': '#e74c3c'}
)

fig4.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    yaxis_tickprefix='€',
    hovermode='x unified'
)

fig4.show()


                            
                                            
3-month revenue forecast using rolling average trend — projected Q1 2025 growth of approximately 6%.

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Summary

Total 2024 Revenue: ~€4.2M across all regions and categories
Top Category: Electronics (37% share)
Top Region: Dublin, followed by Cork
Forecast: Modest Q1 2025 growth projected at ~2% month-on-month







TipBusiness Recommendation



Invest in Electronics inventory heading into Q2, where seasonal demand peaks. Cork’s Food & Beverage performance suggests a targeted regional marketing opportunity.



Analysis by Gnanesh Reddy · MSc Business Analytics, DCU · 20251

title: Sales Revenue Analysis
text:
Footnotes


Dataset is synthetic. Methodology follows standard retail analytics practice using rolling averages for short-term forecasting.↩︎

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
This project applies K-means clustering to segment retail customers based on purchase frequency, recency, and spend — a classic RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) analysis used widely in business intelligence and marketing strategy.






NoteDataset



Synthetically generated customer transaction data modelled on realistic retail distributions. The same pipeline applies directly to real CRM exports.

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
This project applies K-means clustering to segment retail customers based on purchase frequency, recency, and spend — a classic RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) analysis used widely in business intelligence and marketing strategy.






NoteDataset



Synthetically generated customer transaction data modelled on realistic retail distributions. The same pipeline applies directly to real CRM exports.

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
What is RFM Analysis?



Metric
Definition
Business Meaning




Recency
Days since last purchase
How recently did they buy?


Frequency
Number of purchases
How often do they buy?


Monetary
Total spend
How much do they spend?

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Data Generation & Clustering


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

np.random.seed(21)
n = 200

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'CustomerID': range(1, n+1),
    'Recency':    np.random.randint(1, 365, n),
    'Frequency':  np.random.randint(1, 50, n),
    'Monetary':   np.random.randint(100, 5000, n)
})

scaler = StandardScaler()
scaled = scaler.fit_transform(df[['Recency', 'Frequency', 'Monetary']])

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=4, random_state=42, n_init=10)
df['Segment'] = kmeans.fit_predict(scaled)

segment_names = {0: 'High Value', 1: 'At Risk', 2: 'Loyal', 3: 'New Customers'}
df['Segment'] = df['Segment'].map(segment_names)

print("Segment Averages:")
print(df.groupby('Segment')[['Recency', 'Frequency', 'Monetary']].mean().round(1))


Segment Averages:
               Recency  Frequency  Monetary
Segment                                    
At Risk          238.7       16.2     891.5
High Value       239.4       14.3    3960.9
Loyal            265.4       39.7    2347.5
New Customers     71.8       29.2    2797.5

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
3D Segment Visualisation


Code
fig = px.scatter_3d(
    df,
    x='Recency',
    y='Frequency',
    z='Monetary',
    color='Segment',
    title='Customer Segments — RFM Clustering',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
    opacity=0.75
)

fig.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            
Four distinct customer segments identified by K-means clustering across Recency, Frequency, and Monetary dimensions.

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Average Spend by Segment


Code
summary = df.groupby('Segment')['Monetary'].mean().reset_index()
summary.columns = ['Segment', 'Avg Spend (€)']
summary = summary.sort_values('Avg Spend (€)', ascending=False)

fig2 = px.bar(
    summary,
    x='Segment',
    y='Avg Spend (€)',
    color='Segment',
    title='Average Spend by Customer Segment',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
    text_auto='.0f'
)

fig2.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    showlegend=False,
    yaxis_tickprefix='€'
)

fig2.show()


                            
                                            
High Value customers spend on average 4x more than New Customers — prioritising retention efforts is critical.

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Segment Size Distribution


Code
size = df['Segment'].value_counts().reset_index()
size.columns = ['Segment', 'Count']

fig3 = px.pie(
    size,
    names='Segment',
    values='Count',
    title='Customer Segment Distribution',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
    hole=0.4
)

fig3.update_traces(textposition='outside', textinfo='percent+label')
fig3.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18
)

fig3.show()


                            
                                            
Segment distribution — understanding the size of each group informs resource allocation for marketing campaigns.

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Business Recommendations






TipStrategic Actions by Segment




 High Value — Reward with a loyalty programme and early access offers
 At Risk — Target with a win-back email campaign and discount incentives
 Loyal — Upsell premium products and cross-sell complementary items
 New Customers — Onboarding discount to increase purchase frequency

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Key Findings

K-means successfully identifies four behavioural clusters from RFM features
High Value customers represent the smallest segment but drive the most revenue
At Risk customers require immediate re-engagement to prevent churn
This analysis scales directly to real CRM data with minimal modification


Analysis by Gnanesh Reddy · MSc Business Analytics, DCU · 20251

title: Customer Segmentation Analysis
text:
Footnotes


Dataset is synthetically generated. K-means clustering implemented using scikit-learn.↩︎

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
This project analyses simulated S&P 500 sector performance data to identify trends, compare volatility across sectors, and visualise cumulative returns over a 12-month period. The goal is to demonstrate how Python can replace manual spreadsheet analysis for financial decision-making.






NoteDataset



This project uses synthetically generated data modelled on realistic sector return distributions. In a production setting, this pipeline would connect to a financial API such as Yahoo Finance or Alpha Vantage.

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
This project analyses simulated S&P 500 sector performance data to identify trends, compare volatility across sectors, and visualise cumulative returns over a 12-month period. The goal is to demonstrate how Python can replace manual spreadsheet analysis for financial decision-making.






NoteDataset



This project uses synthetically generated data modelled on realistic sector return distributions. In a production setting, this pipeline would connect to a financial API such as Yahoo Finance or Alpha Vantage.

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Setup and Data Generation


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

np.random.seed(42)

sectors = ['Technology', 'Finance', 'Healthcare', 'Energy', 'Consumer']
months = pd.date_range(start='2024-01-01', periods=12, freq='MS')

# Simulate monthly returns (percent)
returns_data = {
    'Technology': [3.2, -1.1, 4.5, 2.8, -0.9, 5.1, 1.3, 3.7, -2.1, 4.2, 2.9, 6.1],
    'Finance':    [1.8, 0.5, 2.1, -0.7, 1.9, 2.4, 0.8, 1.5, 2.2, -1.1, 3.1, 1.7],
    'Healthcare': [0.9, 1.4, -0.3, 2.1, 1.1, 0.7, 2.3, 1.8, 0.5, 2.6, 1.2, 1.9],
    'Energy':     [-2.1, 3.4, 1.7, -1.2, 4.1, -0.8, 2.9, -1.5, 3.2, 1.1, -0.6, 2.3],
    'Consumer':   [1.1, 0.8, 1.9, 1.3, 0.4, 1.7, 0.9, 2.1, 1.4, 0.6, 2.3, 1.5]
}

df_returns = pd.DataFrame(returns_data, index=months)
df_returns.index.name = 'Date'

print("Monthly Returns Summary:")
print(df_returns.describe().round(2))


Monthly Returns Summary:
       Technology  Finance  Healthcare  Energy  Consumer
count       12.00    12.00       12.00   12.00     12.00
mean         2.47     1.35        1.35    1.04      1.33
std          2.63     1.26        0.84    2.18      0.60
min         -2.10    -1.10       -0.30   -2.10      0.40
25%          0.75     0.73        0.85   -0.90      0.88
50%          3.05     1.75        1.30    1.40      1.35
75%          4.28     2.12        1.95    2.98      1.75
max          6.10     3.10        2.60    4.10      2.30

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Monthly Returns by Sector


Code
df_melted = df_returns.reset_index().melt(id_vars='Date', var_name='Sector', value_name='Return (%)')

fig = px.line(
    df_melted,
    x='Date',
    y='Return (%)',
    color='Sector',
    markers=True,
    title='Monthly Sector Returns — 2024',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2
)

fig.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    legend_title_text='Sector',
    xaxis_title='Month',
    yaxis_title='Return (%)',
    hovermode='x unified'
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            
Monthly returns (%) across five S&P 500 sectors — January to December 2024

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Cumulative Returns


Code
df_cumulative = (1 + df_returns / 100).cumprod() - 1
df_cumulative = df_cumulative * 100

df_cum_melted = df_cumulative.reset_index().melt(id_vars='Date', var_name='Sector', value_name='Cumulative Return (%)')

fig2 = px.area(
    df_cum_melted,
    x='Date',
    y='Cumulative Return (%)',
    color='Sector',
    title='Cumulative Sector Returns — 2024',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2
)

fig2.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    hovermode='x unified'
)

fig2.show()


                            
                                            
Cumulative returns show Technology significantly outperforming other sectors by year-end.

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Volatility Comparison


Code
volatility = df_returns.std().reset_index()
volatility.columns = ['Sector', 'Volatility (std dev)']
mean_return = df_returns.mean().reset_index()
mean_return.columns = ['Sector', 'Mean Monthly Return (%)']

summary = volatility.merge(mean_return, on='Sector')

fig3 = px.scatter(
    summary,
    x='Volatility (std dev)',
    y='Mean Monthly Return (%)',
    text='Sector',
    size='Mean Monthly Return (%)',
    color='Sector',
    title='Risk vs Return by Sector',
    template='plotly_white',
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2
)

fig3.update_traces(textposition='top center')
fig3.update_layout(
    font_family='Source Sans 3',
    title_font_size=18,
    showlegend=False
)

fig3.show()


                            
                                            
Technology shows the highest return but also the highest volatility — a classic risk/return tradeoff.

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Key Findings



Sector
Mean Monthly Return
Volatility
12-Month Cumulative




Technology
Highest
Highest
~31%

TipBusiness Insight



For a risk-averse investor, Healthcare and Consumer sectors offer consistent, low-volatility returns. For growth-focused portfolios, Technology delivers superior cumulative returns despite short-term swings.

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis
text:
Conclusion
This analysis demonstrates how Python and Plotly can automate sector performance reporting that would otherwise require manual Excel work. The same pipeline scales to live data with minimal modification — replacing the synthetic dataset with API calls to a financial data provider.

Code written by Gnanesh Reddy. Dataset is synthetically generated for academic purposes.1

title: Stock Market Trend Analysis

In a production environment, this analysis would use live data from Yahoo Finance or Alpha Vantage.↩︎
